description: Migration to introduce range partitioning by created_at on the documents table. Review the partition key choice, and whether it serves both the technical and tenant-isolation requirements.
review_checklist: Identify partition key choice vs query patterns and tenant isolation
Check rollover plan for new time partitions
Audit migration data-move strategy (this script doesn't include it)

ground_truth.description: Partition key choice (created_at) is wrong for this workload. The table's query patterns are 90% tenant-scoped: `WHERE tenant_id = ? AND ...`. Range-by-time forces every tenant query to touch every partition (no partition pruning). Worse: writes pile into the LATEST partition (current month), creating a hot partition while older partitions sit cold. Correct choice: PARTITION BY HASH (tenant_id) with 32-128 partitions for even write distribution, OR PARTITION BY LIST (tenant_id) for per-tenant isolation of large enterprise tenants.

Pareto-skewed workload (top 50 of 5000 tenants = 80% of writes) is the textbook case for hybrid partitioning: list-partition the top 50 enterprise tenants into dedicated partitions, hash-partition the remaining 4950 into 16-32 buckets. Pure time-range guarantees the worst possible balance: top tenants saturate the latest partition.

PRIMARY KEY (id, created_at) is a structural anti-pattern made necessary by the partition key choice — partition key MUST be in the PK for partition pruning. This means id alone is no longer unique; FK references from document_versions(document_id) and document_collaborators(document_id) BREAK because they reference id only. The migration is fundamentally incomplete: dependent tables need either (id, created_at) FKs or a different partitioning approach.

idx_documents_new_tenant is a partitioned (global-style) index, but PG implements it as one local index per partition. Queries with `WHERE tenant_id = ?` (no created_at filter) must probe ALL partitions' indexes — N times the latency. With time-range partitioning + tenant-scoped queries, this design is doubly bad.

Only three partitions defined (2024-2026). No automated partition rollover plan: when 2027 data arrives, INSERTs FAIL (no matching partition). Use pg_partman or a scheduled migration that creates partitions ahead of time. Migration as written is a ticking time bomb.

Migration creates documents_new alongside documents but provides no data-move plan, no FK update plan, no application cutover step. Migration is unfinished. Required additional steps: (1) backfill from documents → documents_new in batches, (2) trigger to dual-write during cutover, (3) atomic rename swap, (4) update FK targets in dependent tables.

Architectural review: with 5K tenants and Pareto-skewed traffic, single-cluster shared schema is approaching its limits. Consider a 'cell-based' architecture: enterprise tenants get dedicated clusters, free/pro tenants share a multi-tenant cluster. Application routes by tenant_id at the connection layer. This eliminates the partition-key dilemma entirely.
